questions.question: Explain the basic operations performed on a queue data structure. Also state the time complexity of each operation.
keyPoints: Enqueue: Insert element at rear end - O(1)
Dequeue: Remove element from front end - O(1)
Front/Peek: View front element without removal - O(1)
Rear: View rear element - O(1)
isEmpty: Check if queue is empty - O(1)
isFull: Check if queue is full - O(1)

questions.question: What is a circular queue? How does it overcome the limitations of a linear queue? Explain with the condition for 'queue full'.
keyPoints: Circular queue connects last position back to first forming a circle
Overcomes phantom overflow problem of linear queue
In linear queue, after deletions front moves ahead but cannot reuse space
Circular queue wraps rear pointer around using modulo operation
Queue full condition: (rear + 1) % MAX_SIZE == front
One slot is kept empty to distinguish full from empty

questions.question: Write an algorithm to implement enqueue and dequeue operations in a queue using linked list. Also mention the advantages of linked list implementation over array implementation.
keyPoints: Enqueue: Create new node, add at rear end, update rear pointer
Dequeue: Remove node from front end, update front pointer, free memory
Advantages: Dynamic size (no overflow), memory efficient (no pre-allocation), no wastage of space
Disadvantages: Extra memory for pointers, not cache-friendly

questions.question: Differentiate between Simple Queue, Priority Queue, and Deque with suitable examples.
keyPoints: Simple Queue: FIFO order - waiting line for tickets
Priority Queue: Elements removed by priority, not FIFO - emergency room triage
Deque: Insert/delete from both ends - can work as queue or stack
Simple queue has single insertion end (rear) and single deletion end (front)
Deque allows operations at both front and rear

questions.question: Explain the queue underflow and overflow conditions. How are they handled in implementation?
keyPoints: Overflow: Attempt to add element when queue is full - cannot happen in linked list
Underflow: Attempt to remove element when queue is empty
Handling: Check conditions before operations
In array: Check (rear == MAX_SIZE - 1) for overflow
In array: Check (front == -1) for underflow
Proper error messages or exceptions are thrown

questions.question: State and explain the applications of queue data structure in computer science.
keyPoints: CPU/Process Scheduling: Ready queue in OS for scheduling processes
Breadth-First Search (BFS): Queue used to traverse graphs level by level
Print Queue: Multiple print jobs queued for printer
Request Handling: Web servers use queue to handle incoming requests
Breadth-First Traversal in trees
Simulation and modeling

questions.question: A circular queue of size 5 contains elements at positions: front=2, rear=4. Draw the queue state and calculate the number of elements currently present.
keyPoints: Draw array indices 0 to 4
Front pointer at index 2, Rear at index 4
Number of elements = (rear - front + N) % N = (4-2+5)%5 = 2 elements
Elements are at positions 2 and 3 (or indices front to rear)
Show circular wrap-around concept visually

questions.question: Explain the array implementation of a linear queue showing the problem of 'phantom overflow'. Also explain how circular queue solves this problem with proper diagrams and algorithm.
keyPoints: Linear queue: Front and rear start at -1, increment on operations
Problem: After dequeues, front moves but cannot reuse front space
Example: Array size 5, after 3 enqueues (0,1,2) and 2 dequeues (0,1 removed), front=2, rear=4
Queue appears full (rear=4) but only has 2 elements
Circular queue: Use (rear+1)%N == front for full, front==-1 for empty
Wrap-around using modulo prevents wastage
Draw comparison diagrams showing linear vs circular states

questions.question: Write complete C/Python code for implementing a queue using arrays with operations: enqueue, dequeue, display, isFull, isEmpty. Also analyze the time and space complexity.
keyPoints: Define MAX_SIZE constant
Maintain front and rear pointers initialized to -1
enqueue: Check isFull, increment rear, add element
dequeue: Check isEmpty, get element at front, increment front
display: Loop from front to rear
Time complexity: All operations O(1)
Space complexity: O(n) where n is MAX_SIZE
Handle edge cases: empty queue, full queue, single element

questions.question: Explain the Breadth-First Search (BFS) algorithm using a queue. Trace the algorithm on a sample graph showing the state of queue at each step.
keyPoints: BFS uses queue to explore vertices level by level
Algorithm: Start with source, mark visited, enqueue source
While queue not empty: dequeue, process, enqueue all unvisited neighbors
Initialize: visited array, queue
Trace example graph showing each level
Result: Vertices visited in order of distance from source
Queue contents shown at each iteration

questions.question: Compare and contrast linear queue, circular queue, and linked list based queue implementations. Give advantages and disadvantages of each.
keyPoints: Linear Queue: Simple but inefficient space utilization
Circular Queue: Efficient space but complex implementation
Linked List Queue: Dynamic size, no overflow, but extra pointer memory
Linear/Circular: Fixed size, array-based, cache-friendly
Linked List: Dynamic, no pre-allocation, but pointer overhead
All have O(1) for enqueue/dequeue operations
Choice depends on: size known, memory constraints, performance needs
